problem:
Patient Records: Input:
age: 31. gender: M.  
Name:  ___                Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: SURGERY
 
Allergies: 
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory.

  

 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Abdominal colectomy, ileostomy.

 
History of Present Illness:
___ with medically-refractory ulcerative colitis, who presented 
this admission for total abdominal colectomy with ileoanal pouch 
or end ileostomy.
 
Past Medical History:
PMH: h/o C. Diff colitis ___, Ulcerative Colitis ___

PSH: appendectomy ___, Right hand/finger debridement
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Mother and Brother with IBS. No FH of colon CA, celiac or IBD.  
 
Physical Exam:
98.5  97.7  61  138/72  15  100RA

GEN: NAD, A&Ox3
CV: RRR
PULM: CTAB
ABD: soft, appropriate ___ ttp, non-distended
EXT: WWP
Neuro: grossly intact
Incision: c/d/i, no evidence of infection
Ostomy: healthy-appearing stoma, soft green stool and gas 
 
Pertinent Results:
___ 05:02AM BLOOD WBC-16.8*# RBC-4.25* Hgb-12.7* Hct-39.7* 
MCV-93 MCH-29.9 MCHC-32.1 RDW-13.6 Plt ___
___ 05:02AM BLOOD Glucose-95 UreaN-9 Creat-1.1 Na-135 K-4.1 
Cl-102 HCO3-27 AnGap-10
___ 05:03AM BLOOD Calcium-8.0* Phos-2.7 Mg-1.8

___ Pathology 
DIAGNOSIS:  
1. Abdominal colon, colectomy (A-L): 
Chronic active colitis. Unremarkable ileum. 
2. Sigmoid colon, colectomy (M-X): 
Chronic active colitis.  
3. Omentum, resection (Y-AA): 
Unremarkable omentum. 
Note: Mucosal granulomas are present focally related to injured 
crypts. This finding is not specific and may be related to crypt 
rupture in any inflammatory condition, including ulcerative 
colitis. No fissures or transmural inflammation are seen. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Patient was brought to the pre-operative area, evaluated by 
anesthesia, and taken to the OR on ___ and underwent a 
total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy as the small bowel 
was unable to be brought down for J-pouch (please refer to 
operative note for complete detail). Patient tolerated to the 
procedure well and was brought to the PACU for a short recovery 
before being brought to the floor.  

Neuro: Post operatively the patient received an epidural with 
good effect and adequate pain control of operative sight but had 
reports of shoulder pain and was started on IV tylenol.  Patient 
continued to report pain and was started on toradol on POD 1 for 
a 3 day coarse with good effect.  On POD1 patient reported some 
right leg motor weakness when attempting to ambulate and 
epidural was turned down by the Acute Pain Service with 
improvement of function, with full restoration after epidural 
was discontinued.  On POD 2 when tolerating oral intake, the 
patient was transitioned to PO oxycodone and tylenol with good 
effect.

CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored.  Patient 
reported shoulder pain post-operatively that radiated into his 
chest and an EKG was obtained which was sinus rhythm without 
evidence of ischemia, with a few PVCs.  Electrolytes were 
normal.  Pain was noted to be sharp in character and unrelated 
to exertion.

Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.

GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced to sips on POD1, and to full liquids 
on POD2, which was well tolerated.  Patient began to have stool 
and gas in ostomy and diet was advanced to POD2 to a regular 
diet.  Ostomy continued to put out gas and stool throughout this 
admission but was not putting out large volumes.  Patient was 
kept until POD 5 to continue ostomy teaching and to monitor 
ostomy output.  Patient's intake and output were closely 
monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary.  
Electrolytes were routinely followed, and repleted when 
necessary.  Patient retained a foley with his epidural, and 
foley was discontinued on POD2 after epidural was pulled and 
patient voided without incident.

ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were 
none.  The incision remained clean, dry, and intact throughout 
this admission. 

Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly.

Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required.

Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and 
venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to get 
up and ambulate as early as possible.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs.  The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding well, and pain was well controlled.  
The patient received discharge teaching and ___ 
instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with 
the discharge plan.  Patient was set up with a visiting nurse 
and stated ___ continue to follow up with the ostomy team 
for his continued care. 
 
Medications on Admission:
MVI, probiotics
 
Discharge Medications:
1. acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every 
6 hours).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
2. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO every ___ hours as 
needed for pain.
Disp:*40 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service
 
Facility:
___
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory.

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
___ were admitted to the inpatient general surgery unit. ___ 
have adequate pain control and have a tolerated a regular diet 
and may go home to continue your recovery.  

Please continue to monitor your ileostomy output. The most 
common complication from a new ileostomy placement is 
dehydration. The output from the stoma is stool from the small 
intestine and the water content is very high. The stool is no 
longer passing through the large intestine which is where the 
water from the stool is reabsorbed into the body and the stool 
becomes formed. ___ must measure your ileostomy output for the 
next few weeks. The output from the stoma should not be more 
than 1200 cc or less than 500 cc. If ___ find that your output 
has become too much or too little, please call the office for 
advice. The office nurse can recommend medications to increase 
or slow the ileostomy output. Keep yourself well hydrated, if 
___ notice your ileostomy output increasing, take in more 
electrolyte drink such as gatoraide. Please monitor yourself for 
signs and symptoms of dehydration including: dizziness 
(especially upon standing), weakness, dry mouth, headache, or 
fatigue. If ___ notice these symptoms please call the office or 
return to the emergency room for evaluation if these symptoms 
are severe. ___ may eat a modified regular diet with your new 
ileostomy.  

Please monitor the appearance of the ostomy and stoma and care 
for it as instructed by the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ stoma 
(intestine that protrudes outside of your abdomen) should be 
beefy red or pink, it may ooze small amounts of blood at times 
when touched and this should subside with time. The skin around 
the ostomy site should be kept clean and intact. Monitor the 
skin around the stoma for buldging or signs of infection listed 
above. Please care for the ostomy as ___ have been instructed by 
the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ will be able to make an appointment 
with the ostomy nurse in the clinic. A visiting nurse ___ who 
will help ___ monitor your ostomy until ___ are comfortable 
caring for it on your own. ___ have a long vertical incision on 
your abdomen that is closed with staples. This incision can be 
left open to air or covered with a dry sterile gauze dressing if 
the staples become irritated from clothing. The staples will 
stay in place until your first post-operative visit at which 
time they can be removed in the clinic, most likely by the 
office nurse. Please monitor the incision for signs and symptoms 
of infection including: increasing redness at the incision, 
opening of the incision, increased pain at the incision line, 
draining of white/green/yellow/foul smelling drainage, or if ___ 
develop a fever. 

Please call the office if ___ develop these symptoms or go to 
the emergency room if the symptoms are severe.  ___ may shower, 
let the warm water run over the incision line and pat the area 
dry with a towel, do not rub. 

No heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks after surgery unless 
instructed otherwise by your surgeon.  

___ will be prescribed a small amount of the pain medication. 
___ may take Tylenol as recommended for pain. Please do not take 
more than 4000 mg of Tylenol daily. Do not drink alcohol while 
taking narcotic pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive 
a car while taking narcotic pain medication. 

Thank ___ for allowing us to participate in your care. Our hope 
is that ___ will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. Best wishes.
 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's answer appears to follow a normal flow in synthesizing a comprehensive discharge summary. There is no indication of backtracking or exploring alternative paths; instead, the information is sequentially presented. All parts of the synthesis contribute cumulatively to the final summary, so none of the content should be removed. I also need to add '\n' to split each reasoning step.
response:
Patient: 31-year-old male
Primary Diagnosis: Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory.
Major Procedure: Total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy (performed on admission day; J-pouch was not feasible due to insufficient small bowel reach).
Significant Findings:
- Pathology: Confirmed chronic active colitis in the abdominal and sigmoid colon. Focal mucosal granulomas were present (attributed to crypt rupture), but the absence of transmural inflammation or fissures supported the diagnosis of ulcerative colitis.
- Laboratory Results: Post-operative leukocytosis (WBC 16.8), mild anemia (Hgb 12.7, Hct 39.7), and slightly low calcium (8.0).
- Clinical Events: Post-operatively, the patient experienced transient right leg weakness due to an epidural, which resolved fully upon adjustment and discontinuation. Referred shoulder pain was evaluated with an EKG, which was negative for ischemia.
Hospital Course:
The patient's recovery was overall stable. His diet was advanced from sips on POD 1 to a regular diet by POD 2 as bowel function returned. He received extensive ostomy teaching and demonstrated competence in management. By POD 5, he was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, ambulating independently, and voiding well.
Discharge Disposition & Instructions:
The patient was discharged home with visiting nurse services. Key instructions include:
- Monitoring ileostomy output (target: 500-1200 cc daily) and maintaining hydration (e.g., electrolyte drinks) to prevent dehydration.
- Incision care for abdominal staples, which are to be removed at the first post-operative visit.
- Activity restriction: No heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks.
- Medications: Acetaminophen and Oxycodone for pain management.
- Follow-up: Scheduled with the surgical and ostomy teams.
candidate:
The objective is to synthesize a comprehensive discharge summary for a 31-year-old male patient hospitalized for medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis, integrating information from the diagnosis/procedures, laboratory/pathology results, and the clinical hospital course. Key elements to include are the primary diagnosis, the surgical procedure (total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy), relevant pathological findings (chronic active colitis), significant post-operative events (epidural-related leg weakness, cardiac-negative shoulder pain), and the discharge status/instructions.

Answer:Comprehensive Discharge Summary:

Patient: 31-year-old male
Primary Diagnosis: Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory.
Major Procedure: Total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy (performed on admission day; J-pouch was not feasible due to insufficient small bowel reach).

Significant Findings:
- Pathology: Confirmed chronic active colitis in the abdominal and sigmoid colon. Focal mucosal granulomas were present (attributed to crypt rupture), but the absence of transmural inflammation or fissures supported the diagnosis of ulcerative colitis.
- Laboratory Results: Post-operative leukocytosis (WBC 16.8), mild anemia (Hgb 12.7, Hct 39.7), and slightly low calcium (8.0).
- Clinical Events: Post-operatively, the patient experienced transient right leg weakness due to an epidural, which resolved fully upon adjustment and discontinuation. Referred shoulder pain was evaluated with an EKG, which was negative for ischemia. 

Hospital Course:
The patient's recovery was overall stable. His diet was advanced from sips on POD 1 to a regular diet by POD 2 as bowel function returned. He received extensive ostomy teaching and demonstrated competence in management. By POD 5, he was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, ambulating independently, and voiding well.

Discharge Disposition & Instructions:
The patient was discharged home with visiting nurse services. Key instructions include:
- Monitoring ileostomy output (target: 500-1200 cc daily) and maintaining hydration (e.g., electrolyte drinks) to prevent dehydration.
- Incision care for abdominal staples, which are to be removed at the first post-operative visit.
- Activity restriction: No heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks.
- Medications: Acetaminophen and Oxycodone for pain management.
- Follow-up: Scheduled with the surgical and ostomy teams.

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's answer involves synthesizing key diagnostic information and significant results while excluding specific sections like 'Discharge Instructions', 'Social History', and 'Medications'. The reasoning is sequential and provides a comprehensive summary of the primary diagnosis, surgical procedures, pathological findings, significant lab results, and clinical course. It follows a normal flow without any backtracking or alternative solution paths.
response:
The patient is a 31-year-old male admitted for medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis. 
 He underwent a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy; a planned J-pouch was not performed as the small bowel could not be brought down sufficiently. 
 Pathological analysis of the abdominal and sigmoid colon confirmed chronic active colitis with focal mucosal granulomas; the ileum and omentum were unremarkable, and the absence of transmural inflammation supported the diagnosis of ulcerative colitis. 
 Significant laboratory results included an elevated white blood cell count (16.8) and mildly decreased levels of hemoglobin (12.7), hematocrit (39.7), and calcium (8.0). 
 Post-operatively, the patient experienced transient right leg motor weakness related to the epidural, which resolved after the epidural was adjusted and discontinued. 
 An EKG performed for post-operative shoulder and chest pain showed sinus rhythm with rare PVCs but no evidence of ischemia. 
 The patient's hospital course was otherwise stable; he advanced to a regular diet by POD2, and his ileostomy demonstrated healthy function. 
 At the time of discharge, the patient was afebrile, alert, and ambulating independently with well-controlled pain.
candidate:
The objective is to synthesize a final summary of key diagnostic information and significant results based on the provided sub-tasks (1, 2, 3, and 4) while excluding 'Discharge Instructions', 'Social History', and 'Medications'. The summary should cover the primary diagnosis, surgical procedures, pathological findings, significant lab results, and the clinical hospital course.

Answer:The patient is a 31-year-old male admitted for medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis. He underwent a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy; a planned J-pouch was not performed as the small bowel could not be brought down sufficiently. Pathological analysis of the abdominal and sigmoid colon confirmed chronic active colitis with focal mucosal granulomas; the ileum and omentum were unremarkable, and the absence of transmural inflammation supported the diagnosis of ulcerative colitis. Significant laboratory results included an elevated white blood cell count (16.8) and mildly decreased levels of hemoglobin (12.7), hematocrit (39.7), and calcium (8.0). Post-operatively, the patient experienced transient right leg motor weakness related to the epidural, which resolved after the epidural was adjusted and discontinued. An EKG performed for post-operative shoulder and chest pain showed sinus rhythm with rare PVCs but no evidence of ischemia. The patient's hospital course was otherwise stable; he advanced to a regular diet by POD2, and his ileostomy demonstrated healthy function. At the time of discharge, the patient was afebrile, alert, and ambulating independently with well-controlled pain.

necessary.  Patient retained a foley with his epidural, and

patient voided without incident.

none.  The incision remained clean, dry, and intact throughout 
this admission. 

Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly.

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service
 
Facility:

Discharge Diagnosis:

Discharge Instructions:
___ were admitted to the inpatient general surgery unit. ___ 
have adequate pain control and have a tolerated a regular diet 
and may go home to continue your recovery.  

Please continue to monitor your ileostomy output. The most 
common complication from a new ileostomy placement is 
dehydration. The output from the stoma is stool from the small 
intestine and the water content is very high. The stool is no 
longer passing through the large intestine which is where the 
water from the stool is reabsorbed into the body and the stool 
becomes formed. ___ must measure your ileostomy output for the 
next few weeks. The output from the stoma should not be more 
than 1200 cc or less than 500 cc. If ___ find that your output 
has become too much or too little, please call the office for 
advice. The office nurse can recommend medications to increase 
or slow the ileostomy output. Keep yourself well hydrated, if 
___ notice your ileostomy output increasing, take in more 
electrolyte drink such as gatoraide. Please monitor yourself for 
signs and symptoms of dehydration including: dizziness 
(especially upon standing), weakness, dry mouth, headache, or 
fatigue. If ___ notice these symptoms please call the office or 
return to the emergency room for evaluation if these symptoms 
are severe. ___ may eat a modified regular diet with your new 
ileostomy.  

Please monitor the appearance of the ostomy and stoma and care 
for it as instructed by the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ stoma 
(intestine that protrudes outside of your abdomen) should be 
beefy red or pink, it may ooze small amounts of blood at times 
when touched and this should subside with time. The skin around 
the ostomy site should be kept clean and intact. Monitor the 
skin around the stoma for buldging or signs of infection listed 
above. Please care for the ostomy as ___ have been instructed by 
the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ will be able to make an appointment 
with the ostomy nurse in the clinic. A visiting nurse ___ who 
will help ___ monitor your ostomy until ___ are comfortable 
caring for it on your own. ___ have a long vertical incision on 
your abdomen that is closed with staples. This incision can be 
left open to air or covered with a dry sterile gauze dressing if 
the staples become irritated from clothing. The staples will 
stay in place until your first post-operative visit at which 
time they can be removed in the clinic, most likely by the 
office nurse. Please monitor the incision for signs and symptoms 
of infection including: increasing redness at the incision, 
opening of the incision, increased pain at the incision line, 
draining of white/green/yellow/foul smelling drainage, or if ___ 
develop a fever. 

Please call the office if ___ develop these symptoms or go to 
the emergency room if the symptoms are severe.  ___ may shower, 
let the warm water run over the incision line and pat the area 
dry with a towel, do not rub. 

No heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks after surgery unless 
instructed otherwise by your surgeon.  

___ will be prescribed a small amount of the pain medication. 
___ may take Tylenol as recommended for pain. Please do not take 
more than 4000 mg of Tylenol daily. Do not drink alcohol while 
taking narcotic pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive 
a car while taking narcotic pain medication. 

Thank ___ for allowing us to participate in your care. Our hope 
is that ___ will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. Best wishes.

___

Patient was brought to the pre-operative area, evaluated by

standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.

fluids. Diet was advanced to sips on POD1, and to full liquids 
on POD2, which was well tolerated.  Patient began to have stool 
and gas in ostomy and diet was advanced to POD2 to a regular 
diet.  Ostomy continued to put out gas and stool throughout this 
admission but was not putting out large volumes.  Patient was 
kept until POD 5 to continue ostomy teaching and to monitor 
ostomy output.  Patient's intake and output were closely 
monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary.  
Electrolytes were routinely followed, and repleted when 
necessary.  Patient retained a foley with his epidural, and 
foley was discontinued on POD2 after epidural was pulled and 
patient voided without incident.

ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were 
none.  The incision remained clean, dry, and intact throughout 
this admission. 

Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly.

Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required.

Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and 
venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to get 
up and ambulate as early as possible.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs.  The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding well, and pain was well controlled.

instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking:
The student's initial answer includes a summary of various findings and events during and after the surgery for ulcerative colitis. However, the final answer removes specific details about Summary 0Wyg because it seems to integrate that information into a comprehensive and succinct narrative of the patient's treatment and recovery. I need to make sure that the distinct steps in reasoning are maintained and that irrelevant or alternative explanations are excluded. I also need to add '
' to split each reasoning step.
response:
Medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis was treated via total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy, with surgical pathology confirming chronic active colitis in the abdominal and sigmoid colon and an unremarkable ileum and omentum.
 Significant postoperative laboratory findings included a white blood cell count of 16.8* k/uL, hemoglobin of 12.7* g/dL, hematocrit of 39.7%*, and calcium of 8.0* mg/dL.
 An EKG indicated sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions and no evidence of ischemia.
 Clinical recovery was marked by a transient right leg motor weakness associated with epidural placement that fully resolved following discontinuation.
 Gastrointestinal function returned promptly, with a regular diet tolerated by postoperative day two and a functional ileostomy producing appropriate gas and stool output.
 At discharge, the patient was ambulatory and independent, with pain well-controlled on oral medications and instructions to monitor for dehydration and maintain stoma care via visiting nurse services.
candidate:
The patient underwent a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy for medically refractory ulcerative colitis. The pathology confirmed chronic active colitis in the colon with an unremarkable ileum and omentum. Postoperative labs showed leukocytosis (16.8), anemia (Hgb 12.7, Hct 39.7), and mild hypocalcemia (8.0). EKG showed sinus rhythm with PVCs. Clinical course included transient leg weakness from the epidural. I selected Summary 0Wyg because it contains the most comprehensive laboratory results (WBC, Hgb, Hct, Calcium) and includes all the pathology labels (abdominal colon, sigmoid colon, ileum, and omentum) mentioned in the report, while maintaining a professional tone without introductory filler.

Answer:Medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis was treated via total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy, with surgical pathology confirming chronic active colitis in the abdominal and sigmoid colon and an unremarkable ileum and omentum. Significant postoperative laboratory findings included a white blood cell count of 16.8* k/uL, hemoglobin of 12.7* g/dL, hematocrit of 39.7%*, and calcium of 8.0* mg/dL. An EKG indicated sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions and no evidence of ischemia. Clinical recovery was marked by a transient right leg motor weakness associated with epidural placement that fully resolved following discontinuation. Gastrointestinal function returned promptly, with a regular diet tolerated by postoperative day two and a functional ileostomy producing appropriate gas and stool output. At discharge, the patient was ambulatory and independent, with pain well-controlled on oral medications and instructions to monitor for dehydration and maintain stoma care via visiting nurse services.

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking:
The student's answer provides a detailed summary of a clinical case. The information is presented in a sequential manner without backtracking or alternative solutions. Therefore, it follows a normal flow. I need to add '
' to split each step.
response:
Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory, was managed with a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy after anatomical constraints precluded J-pouch construction.
 Post-operative surgical pathology confirmed chronic active colitis of the abdominal and sigmoid colon with focal mucosal granulomas attributed to crypt rupture, while the ileum and omentum were unremarkable.
 Significant laboratory results included WBC-16.8*#, RBC-4.25*, Hgb-12.7*, Hct-39.7*, and Calcium-8.0*.
 Evaluation for post-operative shoulder and chest pain via EKG demonstrated sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions and no evidence of ischemia.
 Although the post-operative course was notable for transient right leg motor weakness secondary to epidural anesthesia, the deficit fully resolved upon discontinuation of the epidural.

The objective is to synthesize a professional summary of key diagnostic information and significant results from the provided patient records and sub-tasks. The final response must be a single paragraph starting directly with clinical facts, avoiding introductory phrases like 'The patient is...' or 'In summary...'. I will include the primary diagnosis, the surgical procedure (total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy), pathology findings (chronic active colitis, unremarkable ileum/omentum), significant laboratory values (WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, Calcium), EKG results, and the clinical recovery course leading to discharge.

Answer:Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory, was managed with a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy after anatomical constraints precluded J-pouch construction. Post-operative surgical pathology confirmed chronic active colitis of the abdominal and sigmoid colon with focal mucosal granulomas attributed to crypt rupture, while the ileum and omentum were unremarkable. Significant laboratory results included WBC-16.8*#, RBC-4.25*, Hgb-12.7*, Hct-39.7*, and Calcium-8.0*. Evaluation for post-operative shoulder and chest pain via EKG demonstrated sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions and no evidence of ischemia. Although the post-operative course was notable for transient right leg motor weakness secondary to epidural anesthesia, the deficit fully resolved upon discontinuation of the epidural. By discharge, the patient was afebrile, tolerating a regular diet, and independent in activities of daily living with a functioning end ileostomy.
